error.signature: AI fails to understand Turkish hospitality obligations and tea culture

verdict.summary: Turkish culture has deep hospitality obligations (misafirperverlik). Refusing tea, declining invitations, or rushing through social rituals is deeply offensive. The host's honor depends on the guest's comfort.

dead_ends.action: Refuse offered tea (çay) in Turkish business or social settings
dead_ends.why_fails: Tea is the foundation of Turkish social interaction. Refusing çay is refusing the relationship. Even if you don't drink tea, accept the glass and hold it. Turks drink 6+ cups daily — it's not optional.

dead_ends.action: Decline a Turkish meal invitation or leave early
dead_ends.why_fails: A Turkish host's honor (şeref) depends on making guests comfortable. Declining an invitation after it's sincerely offered, or leaving before the host indicates it's time, is a personal insult.

dead_ends.action: Insist on splitting the bill at a Turkish dinner
dead_ends.why_fails: In Turkey, the person who invites pays (hesap). Splitting bills is unusual and can insult the host's generosity. Accept graciously and reciprocate by hosting next time.

workarounds.action: Accept tea always. In business meetings, tea arrives automatically — it's not a question. In shops, accepting tea doesn't obligate purchase but does build relationship. Chai bardağı (tulip glass) is held by the rim.
When visiting a Turkish home: bring sweets or flowers (not alcohol unless you know they drink). Remove shoes at the door. Compliment the food effusively. Accept second helpings.

Turkey bridges East and West: secular and religious customs coexist. Check context before assuming. Istanbul business is Western-style; rural Anatolia is traditional. Respect Atatürk — criticism of the founder is taboo and illegal.

frequently_confused_with.distinction: Turkish culture shares some Islamic customs with Arab cultures but has distinct secular traditions from Atatürk's reforms
